risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups." Comment: The paper explicitly states random assignment within strata and uses blocked randomization; although it does not name the exact random-number method, blocked randomization is acceptable here because the sequence is described as random and intended to balance groups within strata.

Summary: The report states that randomization occurred after consent and baseline measurements, and that families were stratified and randomly assigned within strata, but it does not describe any mechanism used to conceal the upcoming assignment from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or any other concealment procedure. Comment: Randomization is reported, but allocation concealment is not, so foreknowledge by recruiters cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Intervention group: Year 1 home visits and group sessions" ... "Control group: education only" ... "During Year 1, control families received educational tool kits and incentives sent home on the same cycle as intervention families, but they did not receive any mentoring." Comment: Participants and personnel could not be blinded to assignment because the intervention involved visible home visits, group sessions, and mentoring versus mailed education only. The outcomes included BMI, diet, physical activity, screen time, and caregiver questionnaires, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowing group allocation and by differential attention/support.

Summary: The study does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded, but the main outcomes relevant to the review were measured with objective methods: BMI/anthropometrics (“Height was measured with a portable stadiometer… Weight was measured with a calibrated electronic scale”), physical activity by accelerometers, and biochemical outcomes by laboratory measures. These outcomes are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation even if assessors were not blinded. The only clearly subjective outcomes (questionnaires/recalls) were secondary and do not alter the overall judgment for the primary outcome assessment domain.

Summary: The article indicates that follow-up was substantially retained and even notes the study was designed to retain 120 families from a recruitment goal of 150, with 67 intervention and 83 control families enrolled. It also acknowledges that 2-year retention is difficult, but does not report a pattern of differential dropout, outcome-related exclusions, or other attrition problems suggesting bias. Comment: Although exact per-arm loss-to-follow-up numbers are not clearly reported in the provided text, the overall picture is of a reasonably conducted RCT with planned retention and no obvious imbalance or biased missing-data handling, so Low risk is appropriate under a permissive Cochrane judgement.
